question_text: Let p and q be two propositions. Consider the following two formulae in prepositional logic. $ \pmb { S } _ { 1 } : ( \neg p \land ( p \lor q ) ) p $ $ \pmb { S } _ { 2 } : q ( \neg p \land ( p \lor q ) ) $ Which one of the following choices is correct?

options.C: $S _ { 1 }$ is a tautology but $S _ { 2 }$ is not a tautology.
options.D: $S _ { 1 }$ is not a tautology but $S _ { 2 }$ is a tautology.

solution:
To determine which of the given formulae \( S_1 \) and \( S_2 \) is a tautology, we need to analyze their truth values for all possible combinations of truth values of the propositions \( p \) and \( q \).

Let's start with \( S_1 \):
\[ S_1: (\neg p \land (p \lor q)) p \]

We will construct a truth table for \( S_1 \):

\[
\begin{array}{cc|c|c|c}
p & q & \neg p & p \lor q & \neg p \land (p \lor q) & (\neg p \land (p \lor q)) p \\
\hline
T & T & F & T & F & F \\
T & F & F & T & F & F \\
F & T & T & T & T & F \\
F & F & T & F & F & F \\
\end{array}
\]

From the truth table, we can see that \( S_1 \) is false for all possible combinations of \( p \) and \( q \). Therefore, \( S_1 \) is not a tautology.

Next, let's analyze \( S_2 \):
\[ S_2: q (\neg p \land (p \lor q)) \]

We will construct a truth table for \( S_2 \):

\[
\begin{array}{cc|c|c|c}
p & q & \neg p & p \lor q & \neg p \land (p \lor q) & q (\neg p \land (p \lor q)) \\
\hline
T & T & F & T & F & F \\
T & F & F & T & F & F \\
F & T & T & T & T & T \\
F & F & T & F & F & F \\
\end{array}
\]

From the truth table, we can see that \( S_2 \) is true only when \( p \) is false and \( q \) is true. However, it is not true for all possible combinations of \( p \) and \( q \). Therefore, \( S_2 \) is not a tautology.

Based on the analysis, we can conclude that neither \( S_1 \) nor \( S_2 \) is a tautology.

Thus, the correct answer is:
\[
\boxed{A}
\]
